title: Technique: The Mirror of Self-Criticism
description: This practice helps you notice how harsh your inner critic can be. Through a short role-play, you redirect your usual self-critical phrases toward someone close to you and hear how they sound to someone else. The exercise teaches you to recognize the unfairness of self-devaluation and to develop a kinder attitude toward yourself.

steps: Technique: The Mirror of Self-Criticism
<activitylink id="alkjnaNNasz"></activitylink>
Try one approach: start holding your loved ones to the same standards you usually apply to yourself. Do this honestly.
Very often, within minutes it becomes clear just how harsh and unfair the inner critic can be.
Let’s illustrate this exercise with a dialogue between a client and a psychologist:
<dialog data-psy >Dmitry, you said the layoff left you feeling like a failure and thinking, “I’m a loser; nothing works out for me.”</dialog>

<dialog data-psy >Let’s look at these thoughts from a different angle. I suggest a brief role-play: you’ll repeat your critical phrases, but address them not to yourself—to someone close to you, say your best friend.</dialog>
<dialog data-psy >I’ll play the friend who also lost their job. You’ll play yourself. Ready to try?</dialog>
<dialog  data-man>Yes, let’s try.</dialog>
<dialog data-psy >Let’s begin. I’ll start: “I was fired today.”</dialog>
<dialog  data-man>Maybe… you did something wrong. Otherwise they wouldn’t have fired you.</dialog>
<dialog data-psy >What exactly do you think I did wrong?</dialog>
<dialog  data-man>Well… maybe you made mistakes, weren’t attentive enough.</dialog>
<dialog data-psy >So you’re criticizing me right now?</dialog>
<dialog  data-man>I guess I am. It looks like you weren’t coping. Maybe you’re just not good enough at your job.</dialog>
<dialog data-psy >It’s really painful to hear that. I was hoping for support.</dialog>
<dialog  data-man>I understand… but if you were making mistakes and not coping, how am I supposed to support you?</dialog>
After this role-play, Dmitry said it was very hard to say such things to someone close. He realized he’d never do that in real life—yet he did it to himself all the time.
<q data-author="Brené Brown">Speak to yourself the way you would speak to someone you love.</q>
Interesting, isn’t it? What we could never say to another person, we sometimes say to ourselves with ease.
We often show patience, gentleness, and understanding to others—and forget to show them to ourselves.
Try letting go of the double standard. You also deserve respect, support, and kindness.
Remember: healthy self-esteem is a key foundation for your motivation, goals, and life energy.
With low self-esteem, we underestimate our abilities and almost deprive ourselves of the right to be happy.
Start shifting your attitude toward yourself: notice small wins, allow yourself to be human—to make mistakes and learn. Small steps lead to big changes.
